summary:
The Nahal Mishmar hoard is a collection of 429 objects discovered in 1961 in a cave in the Judean Desert near the Dead Sea, hidden in a rock crevice wrapped in a reed mat and dated to approximately 3500–3000 BCE. The hoard is associated with the [[event:1571|Ghassulian culture]] of the southern Levant and represents the largest and most remarkable assemblage of prehistoric metalwork yet found in the ancient Near East. The objects include mace heads, scepters, crowns, and other ceremonial objects made from copper alloys, as well as a smaller number of ivory and stone objects.

The copper objects in the Nahal Mishmar hoard are extraordinary for their technical sophistication. Many were produced using the lost-wax casting technique — in which a wax model is invested in clay, the wax is melted out, and molten metal poured into the resulting mold. Some objects contain deliberate alloy compositions (with arsenic or antimony) that improved their hardness and appearance, demonstrating that Chalcolithic metallurgists had empirical knowledge of alloying long before the deliberate use of tin-bronze. The objects were clearly ceremonial rather than utilitarian, many representing categories of objects associated with ritual authority — mace heads and scepters were symbols of power in the ancient Near East.

The Nahal Mishmar hoard appears to have been concealed during a period of instability, perhaps hidden for safekeeping and never recovered by its owners. It now resides in the Israel Museum in Jerusalem and stands as a testament to the remarkable metalworking sophistication achieved by Chalcolithic communities of the Levant nearly 5,500 years ago.
